raw_query: SELECT id, title, author_id FROM posts WHERE status = 'PUBLISHED'; [+500 individual SELECT id, name, email FROM authors WHERE id = ?]

columns: author_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES authors(id)

baseline_explain.Analysis: Individual query execution time is ~1ms using Primary Key Index Scans. Total latency is 1250ms strictly due to network overhead and application-layer iteration loops. Database indexes are functioning perfectly.

diagnosis.primary_bottleneck: N+1 Query Pattern: A single API route is issuing 501 discrete database round-trips. One query fetches 500 Post records, then the application iterates and issues 500 individual author lookups inside a for-loop. Each individual query is fast (~1ms on Primary Key), but 500 sequential network round-trips accumulate to ~1250ms of pure latency.
diagnosis.secondary_bottleneck: No use of Prisma's built-in `include` (eager loading) or a batching strategy. The ORM's relation resolution capability is being bypassed entirely in favour of a manual loop.
diagnosis.tertiary_bottleneck: No in-memory caching layer (e.g., DataLoader pattern). If the same authorId appears across multiple posts, duplicate author queries are issued with no deduplication.
diagnosis.worst_node: Application loop: `for (const post of posts) { await prisma.author.findUnique(...) }` — 500 sequential awaits with blocking I/O
diagnosis.database_verdict: NO DATABASE CHANGE REQUIRED. Indexes on `posts.author_id` and `authors.id` are optimal. Adding more indexes will produce zero measurable improvement.

hypotheses.label: Prisma Eager Loading via `include` (Single JOIN Query)
hypotheses.rationale: Replace the 501-query loop with a single Prisma `findMany` call that uses `include: { author: true }`. Prisma translates this to a single SQL LEFT JOIN, fetching all posts and their author data in one round-trip. Eliminates 500 network calls entirely. This is the idiomatic, recommended Prisma solution.
hypotheses.expected_impact: CRITICAL — Reduces 501 queries to 1. Eliminates ~1200ms of network overhead. Final latency: ~50ms.
hypotheses.sql_equivalent: SELECT p.id, p.title, p.status, p.author_id, a.id, a.name, a.email FROM posts p LEFT JOIN authors a ON p.author_id = a.id WHERE p.status = 'PUBLISHED';

hypotheses.label: Prisma Selective Loading via `include` with `select` (Optimized JOIN)
hypotheses.rationale: Same as H1 but adds explicit `select` to return only the fields consumed by the client. Avoids over-fetching on both the posts and authors tables. Minimises serialization cost and network payload size. Recommended for production APIs.
hypotheses.expected_impact: CRITICAL — Same 501→1 query reduction as H1, plus reduced payload and serialization overhead.
hypotheses.sql_equivalent: SELECT p.id, p.title, a.id, a.name, a.email FROM posts p LEFT JOIN authors a ON p.author_id = a.id WHERE p.status = 'PUBLISHED';

hypotheses.label: DataLoader Batching Pattern (Deduplication Cache)
hypotheses.rationale: For GraphQL resolvers or deeply nested relation trees where `include` is impractical, implement a DataLoader-style batch function. Collects all unique authorIds from the posts result, fires a single `prisma.author.findMany({ where: { id: { in: uniqueAuthorIds } } })`, then maps results back. Automatically deduplicates repeated authorIds (e.g., if 10 posts share 1 author, only 1 DB lookup is made for that author). Total queries: 2 regardless of post count.
hypotheses.expected_impact: CRITICAL — Reduces 501 queries to exactly 2. Adds deduplication. Best for dynamic/recursive graphs. ~50-80ms total latency.
hypotheses.sql_equivalent: SELECT id, name, email FROM authors WHERE id IN ('uuid-1', 'uuid-2', ..., 'uuid-N');
